title: Are Large Language Models Good at Utility Judgments?
abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is considered to be a promising approach to alleviate the hallucination issue of large language models (LLMs), and it has received widespread attention from researchers recently. Due to the limitation in the semantic understanding of retrieval models, the success of RAG heavily lies on the ability of LLMs to identify passages with utility. Recent efforts have explored the ability of LLMs to assess the relevance of passages in retrieval, but there has been limited work on evaluating the utility of passages in supporting question answering. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive study about the capabilities of LLMs in utility evaluation for open-domain question answering (QA). Specifically, we introduce a benchmarking procedure and collection of candidate passages with different characteristics, facilitating a series of experiments with five representative LLMs. Our experiments reveal that: (i) well-instructed LLMs can distinguish between relevance and utility, and that LLMs are highly receptive to newly generated counterfactual passages. Moreover, (ii) we scrutinize key factors that affect utility judgments in the instruction design. And finally, (iii) to verify the efficacy of utility judgments in practical retrieval augmentation applications, we delve into LLMs' QA capabilities using the evidence judged with utility and direct dense retrieval results. (iv) We propose a k-sampling, listwise approach to reduce the dependency of LLMs on the sequence of input passages, thereby facilitating subsequent answer generation. We believe that the way we formalize and study the problem along with our findings contributes to a critical assessment of retrieval-augmented LLMs. Our code and benchmark can be found at https://github.com/ict-bigdatalab/utility_judgments.
intro:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is considered a crucial means to effectively mitigate the hallucination issues in LLM [cite], garnering widespread attention from researchers recently [cite]. 
Due to the semantic limitations in the retrieval model's understanding, the practical efficacy of RAG heavily relies on the LLM's ability to accurately identify passages with utility among the retrieved candidate passages. 
Recent studies have explored the capabilities of LLM in relevance judgments during retrieval [cite]. 
However, there has been limited focus on evaluating the utility judgment.

 
 < g r a p h i c s >

 

 An example between utility and relevance.

 

Relevance judgments via LLM 
[cite] investigated the use of LLM to automatically generate relevance judgments in IR. 
Many works have examined the zero-shot language understanding and reasoning capabilities of LLM for relevance ranking, including pointwise [cite], pairwise [cite], and listwise [cite] approaches. 
These publications have shown that LLM excel in judging the relevance of passages to the query and achieve state-of-the-art ranking performance on standard benchmarks [cite]. 

Utility judgments via LLM 
In this paper, we explore whether LLM are good at judging the utility of passages. 
To appreciate the importance of (passage) utility, recall that LLM find extensive use in open-domain QA [cite]. 
A common approach in open-domain QA involves retrieval-augmented LLM [cite], which 
[label=(*)]
 
 * acquires a set of supporting evidence upon which to condition, and
 
 * incorporates the selected evidence into the downstream LLM generation process. 

In open-domain QA, the goal of obtaining supporting evidence differs significantly from the goal of obtaining relevance judgments using LLM: the supporting evidence should align with the judgments made by the LLM regarding which evidence qualifies as having utility for answering the question [cite]. 
Utility and relevance are distinct concepts [cite]:
[label=(*)]
 
 * Relevance signifies a connection between information and a context or question [cite], and 
 
 * utility refers to the practical benefits of downstream tasks derived from consuming the information [cite]. 
 E.g., all evidence in Fig. <ref> has different relevance to the question. 
 However, only “[Evidence-2]” has utility in answering the question and other evidence, although connected to “leaf color change”, lacks useful information on the underlying reasons for this phenomenon.

Research goal
The above observations naturally raise a question: Are LLM not only good at generating relevance judgments but also utility judgments?
To address this question, we undertake an empirical study in the setting of open-domain QA, investigating the capability of different LLM in judging passage utility. Specifically, our study is broken down into three concrete research questions:
[leftmargin=*,label=(RQ*),nosep]
 
 * Can LLM distinguish between utility and relevance?

 
 * What factors affect the ability of LLM to judge the utility of evidence?
 
 
 * How do utility judgments impact the QA abilities of retrieval-augmented LLM? 

Benchmarking procedure 
In this work, we introduce the utility judgments task: 
Given a question and a set of candidate passages, the utility judgments task is to identify supporting evidence with utility in answering the question. 
We use several representative LLM as zero-shot utility judges.

As illustrated in Figure <ref>, we carefully design pointwise, pairwise, and listwise prompting approaches for LLM-based utility judges, as well as QA prompting approaches guiding LLM in answering questions using selected evidence. 

To facilitate the study and evaluation of the utility judgments task, we formulate two hypotheses guiding the construction of novel benchmark datasets in Section <ref>: 
[label=(*)]
 
 * ground-truth inclusion: the set of candidate passages must encompass ground-truth evidence. The ground-truth evidence offers the highest utility for question among the candidate passages; and 
 
 * ground-truth uncertainty: there exists uncertainty regarding the presence of ground-truth evidence in the set of candidate passages. 

Our empirical work leads to the following interesting results: 

 
 * For RQ1: The answer is YES. 
 LLM can distinguish between utility and relevance given candidate passages. 
 
 Specifically, utility judgments may offer more valuable guidance than relevance judgments to LLM in identifying ground-truth evidence necessary for answering questions. 
 Moreover, LLM may exhibit a preference for selecting ground-truth evidence with utility when confronted with entity substitution-based counterfactual passages, compared to generated counterfactual passages. 

 
 * For RQ2: Different LLM exhibit varying capabilities in utility judgment, with ChatGPT standing out as the most powerful. 
 There is a consistent improvement in utility judgments performance the expansion of model scale. 
 Listwises approaches may demonstrate superior performance compared to pointwise and pairwise approaches.
 
 In listwise approaches, LLM are sensitive to the position of the ground-truth evidence in the input list. 
 Moreover, the inclusion of chain-of-thought, reasoning process and answer generation also impact performance.

 
 * For RQ3: Employing LLM as zero-shot utility judges or relevance judges proves more advantageous for answer generation than directly using dense retrieval. 
 The QA performance of LLM is optimal when using evidence with utility judged by LLM. 
 To reduce the dependency of LLM on the position of ground-truth evidence, we propose a k-sampling listwise approach that combines multiple utility judgments results to derive the final outcome, thereby facilitating subsequent answer generation. However, a significant gap still exists when compared to using only ground-truth evidence. 
 

In Section <ref>, we provide a detailed description of the analysis setting. In Section <ref>–<ref>, we address the three proposed research questions based on respective experimental results. Section <ref> discusses related work and conclusions are drawn in Section <ref>.
related_work:
LLM for relevance judgments. 
With exhibited unprecedented proficiency in language understanding, large language models (LLM) such as ChatGPT [cite] and Llama 2 [cite] have seen widespread applications across various tasks [cite]. 
IR is a representative work of LLM applications, with many studies incorporating LLM into relevance ranking [cite]. 
Research into LLM in relevance ranking mainly contains the following three approaches: 
[label=(*)]

 * pointwise [cite],

 * pairwise [cite], and

 * listwise [cite].

[cite] employed LLM in scoring fine-grained pointwise relevance labels.
[cite] employed a pairwise relevance comparison method to distinguish differences between candidate outputs. 
Previous works [cite] analyzed the capabilities of LLM in the relevance ranking task.

[cite] demonstrated LLM' proficiency in relevance assessment in IR. However, relevance in IR and utility in answering specific questions are distinct concepts. This paper investigates whether LLM excel in judging the utility of retrieved passages. Similar to relevance ranking tasks, we devise pointwise, pairwise, and listwise approaches for utility judgments.

Retrieval-augmented LLM for QA
The application of LLM in QA [cite] is mainly retrieval-augmented LLM [cite]. 
Current researches on retrieval-augmented LLM can be categorized into two main groups, i.e., independent architectures [cite] and joint architectures [cite].

In independent architectures, the retriever and LLM operate independently, with the retriever's sole role being to provide relevant external knowledge to the LLM [cite]. 
For example, [cite] demonstrated that using retrieval-augmented methods can improve GPT-3 performance on open-domain question answering. 
However, these retrieval models are usually based on the probability ranking principle (PRP) [cite], ranking passages based on their likelihood of being relevant to the question [cite], which may not align with a retrieval-augmented framework. 
In the joint architecture, the LLM actively engage in the training process of the retriever [cite]. 
[cite] used the performance of the LLM in answer generation as feedback to train the retriever to retrieve the evidence that contribute more utility to answering the question. 

The independent retriever may struggle to align well with the utility requirements of LLM on the retrieval passages. 
Although joint architecture partially alleviates this issue, depending on the answers outputted by LLM as utility judgments for retrieved passages is influenced by the LLM' internal knowledge. 
Since LLM may produce different answers for the same input passages, assessing passage utility based solely on the quality of LLM' answers in joint architecture may not always accurately reflect the passages' inherent utility for answering questions.
Therefore, we directly investigate the LLM's capability of utility judgment. 
We hope our work provides useful insights for understanding and improving retrieval-augmented LLM in the future.
